description: Workspace-scoped coding task runtime persists Codex task metadata, tmux session hints, recoverable states, and append-only run logs.
steps: Run `.venv/bin/pytest tests/coding_tasks/test_store.py tests/coding_tasks/test_manager.py` and verify the persistence and lifecycle tests pass.
Confirm creating a coding task writes `tasks.json` plus a matching `runs/<task_id>.jsonl` entry under the workspace automation directory.
Verify tasks in `starting`, `running`, or `waiting_user` are surfaced as recoverable by the worker manager after reloading from disk.

description: Gateway startup wires the coding task runtime and reports tracked versus recoverable coding tasks without breaking existing startup flow.
steps: Start `nanobot gateway` in a controlled workspace with coding task files present.
Verify startup output reports the number of tracked and recoverable coding tasks alongside existing cron and heartbeat status.
Confirm gateway startup still reaches the normal ready state instead of failing before channel bootstrap.

description: Coding task IDs and default tmux session names are stable, repo-derived, and safe for reuse across restarts.
steps: Create a coding task for a repo with a normal directory name.
Verify the generated tmux session name includes the repo basename and task id in a shell-safe format.
Confirm reloading the stored task preserves the same session name rather than deriving a new one.

description: CLI can create a new coding task record with repo path, goal, and default execution policy.
steps: Run the new CLI command to create a coding task against a sample repo path.
Verify the command returns the task id and initial status.
Confirm the created task is visible in the persisted coding task store.

description: CLI can list coding tasks with status, repo, last progress summary, and recoverability.
steps: Create multiple coding tasks in different statuses.
Run the list command and verify all tasks are shown with status and repo path.
Confirm recoverable tasks are visually distinguished from queued, completed, and failed tasks.

description: CLI can show detailed status for a single coding task including session metadata and recent run events.
steps: Create a coding task and move it through at least one state transition.
Run the detailed status command for that task id.
Verify the response includes tmux session, last progress summary, and recent run-log events.

description: CLI can cancel a coding task and persist the cancellation reason to the task run log.
steps: Create a queued or running coding task.
Issue the CLI cancellation command with a short reason.
Verify the task status becomes `cancelled` and the reason is visible in the run history.

description: CLI can resume a failed or waiting coding task by moving it back into the startup path for the same worker session.
steps: Prepare a task in `failed` or `waiting_user` state.
Run the CLI resume command for that task id.
Verify the task transitions back to `starting` instead of creating a duplicate task record.

description: Telegram private-chat command parsing can create a coding task from a natural-language '开始编程' request.
steps: Send a private Telegram message that requests starting a coding task against a repo.
Verify nanobot creates exactly one coding task record with the parsed repo and goal.
Confirm the chat receives an acknowledgement containing the task id and initial state.

description: Telegram private-chat control messages map onto the active coding task lifecycle instead of being treated as normal chat.
steps: Start a coding task from Telegram private chat.
Send control messages such as `状态`, `继续`, and `取消` in the same chat.
Verify nanobot routes those controls to the tracked coding task instead of answering with generic assistant text.

description: Nanobot enforces a single active coding task per workspace in the MVP so Telegram control messages stay unambiguous.
steps: Start one coding task and leave it running.
Attempt to start a second coding task in the same workspace.
Verify nanobot refuses the second start or queues it explicitly instead of launching both concurrently.

description: Repo path validation rejects missing paths, non-directories, and paths outside the allowed local filesystem expectations before task creation.
steps: Attempt to create coding tasks for a missing path and a file path.
Verify nanobot rejects each invalid target with a clear explanation.
Confirm no task record is created for rejected inputs.

description: Harness detection can identify whether a target repo already has `PLAN.json`, `PROGRESS.md`, or `init.sh` before Codex is launched.
steps: Create one repo fixture with harness files and one without.
Run the harness detection logic for both repos.
Verify the detected state distinguishes existing harnesses from missing ones.

description: Prompt building for existing-harness repos tells Codex to restore state first and honor the repo's startup sequence before editing.
steps: Build the initial Codex prompt for a repo that already contains harness files.
Verify the prompt explicitly instructs Codex to read `PROGRESS.md`, `PLAN.json`, and run the repo init sequence first.
Confirm the prompt includes the user goal without overwriting the repo's own instructions.

description: Prompt building for repos without a harness tells Codex to initialize the harness before starting feature work.
steps: Build the initial Codex prompt for a repo that does not have harness files.
Verify the prompt instructs Codex to generate `PLAN.json`, `PROGRESS.md`, and `init.sh` before coding.
Confirm the prompt still includes the requested task goal and execution boundaries.

description: Codex launcher can start a new tmux-backed worker session for a coding task and persist the tmux target back into task state.
steps: Create a coding task and launch the Codex worker.
Verify a tmux session is created or attached for that task id.
Confirm the task state records the tmux session and moves to `starting`.

description: Codex launcher can reuse an existing tmux session for the same task instead of spawning duplicate worker shells.
steps: Launch a coding task once to create its tmux session.
Trigger resume or continue for the same task.
Verify nanobot writes into the original tmux target instead of creating a second session.

description: Codex startup command carries repo path, task goal, and execution boundaries into the worker in a reproducible way.
steps: Launch a coding task and inspect the command or prompt sent into the Codex worker.
Verify the repo path, goal, and no-push execution boundary are all present.
Confirm repeated launches for the same task preserve the same goal and repo routing.

description: Codex session hints extracted from the running worker can be persisted for later resume and diagnosis.
steps: Launch a coding task and capture the first visible Codex session metadata or banner output.
Verify nanobot stores a session hint in the coding task record when that information is present.
Confirm the stored hint survives reloading the coding task from disk.

description: Worker polling can capture recent tmux pane output for a running Codex task without blocking the gateway event loop.
steps: Run a coding task long enough to produce several lines of output in tmux.
Invoke the pane polling logic while the task continues running.
Verify nanobot retrieves recent output without freezing cron, heartbeat, or chat handling.

description: Pane-output polling updates the coding task's last progress summary and timestamp when visible work advances.
steps: Poll a running coding task after new terminal output appears.
Verify the task's last progress summary changes to reflect the new work.
Confirm the progress timestamp updates at the same time.

description: Progress summarization can read the target repo's `PROGRESS.md` and extract the latest session note for reporting.
steps: Prepare a repo with a populated `PROGRESS.md` containing multiple session entries.
Run the progress summarizer against that repo.
Verify the latest session note appears in the coding task report.

description: Progress summarization can read the target repo's `PLAN.json` and compute completed versus remaining feature counts.
steps: Prepare a repo with a `PLAN.json` containing a mix of passing and pending features.
Run the summarizer against that repo.
Verify the resulting report includes accurate completed and remaining counts.

description: Nanobot can synthesize harness files and terminal output into a concise progress report for a coding task.
steps: Run a coding task in a repo that has both harness files and recent worker output.
Request task status through the reporting path.
Verify the response includes what was completed, what is in progress, and the next blocker or next step.

description: Gateway restart recovery can reload coding task state and reconnect to tasks left in `starting`, `running`, or `waiting_user`.
steps: Leave a coding task in a recoverable state and restart the gateway.
Verify the gateway reloads the task state from disk on startup.
Confirm the task becomes observable again without creating a duplicate record.

description: If a recoverable task's tmux session is missing after restart, nanobot marks the task failed with an actionable recovery hint.
steps: Delete or kill the tmux session for a recoverable coding task before gateway restart.
Restart the gateway and trigger recovery scanning.
Verify the task moves to `failed` and the status message explains how to resume or relaunch it.

description: Telegram `状态` requests return the active coding task summary rather than a generic assistant answer.
steps: Start a coding task from Telegram private chat.
Send `状态` while the task is active.
Verify the reply contains task status, repo, last progress, and recoverability.

description: Telegram `继续` requests send a control instruction into the same Codex worker session for the active task.
steps: Start a coding task and let it reach a paused or waiting state.
Send `继续` from the same Telegram private chat.
Verify nanobot writes the resume instruction into the existing worker session instead of creating a new task.

description: Telegram `停止` requests can gracefully interrupt the running Codex worker while preserving task state for later resume.
steps: Run a coding task until the worker is actively doing work.
Send `停止` from Telegram private chat.
Verify the worker is interrupted and the task status becomes `waiting_user` or another resumable state.

description: Telegram `取消` requests fully cancel the coding task and prevent later resume without an explicit relaunch.
steps: Start a coding task and then send `取消` in Telegram.
Verify the task status becomes `cancelled` and the run log records the control action.
Confirm later `继续` attempts are rejected for that cancelled task.

description: Coding task creation stores a no-push default approval policy so Codex can commit locally without pushing automatically.
steps: Create a coding task with the default execution policy.
Verify the persisted task record shows the expected no-push or local-only approval policy.
Confirm the initial Codex prompt reflects that boundary.

description: Codex startup prompt explicitly instructs the worker to honor the target repo's `AGENTS.md` and harness startup sequence before editing.
steps: Generate the startup prompt for a repo that contains custom `AGENTS.md` instructions.
Verify the prompt tells Codex to follow repo-specific instructions first.
Confirm the prompt still preserves the user goal and nanobot execution boundary.

description: A repo without an existing harness triggers a Codex harness-init flow before the first implementation step.
steps: Launch a coding task against a repo that lacks `PLAN.json`, `PROGRESS.md`, and `init.sh`.
Verify Codex initializes the harness files before touching implementation files.
Confirm the coding task report reflects that the harness was created first.

description: A repo with an existing harness triggers a Codex recovery flow that reads progress and baseline state before new coding work begins.
steps: Launch a coding task against a repo with an active harness and prior progress entries.
Verify the first visible worker actions are reading `PROGRESS.md`, `PLAN.json`, and running the repo init sequence.
Confirm progress reporting references the pre-existing harness context rather than ignoring it.

description: Every significant coding task lifecycle change appends a structured run event so later sessions can audit what happened.
steps: Create a coding task and move it through multiple states.
Inspect the corresponding `runs/<task_id>.jsonl` file.
Verify each lifecycle change appears as a structured append-only event in chronological order.

description: User control actions such as continue, pause, and cancel are appended to the run log separately from automatic worker status changes.
steps: Send at least two control messages against an active coding task.
Inspect the task's run log.
Verify the control actions are recorded as distinct events rather than being folded into generic status updates.

description: Progress notifications are throttled so nanobot does not spam Telegram while Codex emits many short terminal updates.
steps: Run a coding task that produces frequent worker output over a short period.
Observe the outgoing Telegram progress notifications.
Verify notifications are grouped or throttled instead of sending one message per terminal line.

description: Successful completion reports include task outcome, verification summary, repo branch, and recent commit information.
steps: Complete a coding task that makes at least one commit.
Observe the final completion message sent to Telegram or CLI.
Verify it includes the outcome, test status, branch, and recent commit summary.

description: Failure reports include the last successful step, current blocker, and a concrete resume hint.
steps: Force a coding task into a failure state after some progress has been made.
Request or observe the failure report.
Verify the report includes what completed successfully, what failed, and how to resume.

description: Tasks that need human approval can move into `waiting_user` with a summary that explains what Codex is waiting on.
steps: Run a coding task against a repo whose instructions require a plan confirmation before major edits.
Verify nanobot moves the task into `waiting_user` when that pause point is reached.
Confirm the stored summary explains exactly what confirmation or decision is needed.

description: Task metadata can persist the working branch once Codex creates or switches branches in the target repo.
steps: Run a coding task that creates or switches to a dedicated branch.
Inspect the stored coding task metadata after the branch change.
Verify the branch name is persisted for status and completion reporting.

description: Task metadata can persist a recent commit summary so completion and status messages do not need to rescan git every time.
steps: Run a coding task that makes at least one commit.
Update task metadata from the repo state.
Verify the coding task record stores a recent commit summary that matches `git log --oneline`.

description: Multiple queued coding tasks can survive restart even though only one active task is allowed in the first release.
steps: Create several queued coding tasks without running them all at once.
Restart the gateway or reload the store.
Verify all queued tasks remain intact and none are dropped or duplicated.

description: Status listing cleanly distinguishes queued, running, waiting, completed, failed, and cancelled coding tasks.
steps: Populate the coding task store with tasks in each supported status.
Run the list/status reporting path.
Verify each status appears distinctly and maps to the correct stored task.

description: Workspace artifacts directory can store auxiliary coding-task material such as prompt snapshots or progress extracts used for reporting.
steps: Run a coding task that emits at least one saved artifact.
Inspect the workspace automation artifacts directory after the run.
Verify the saved artifact is namespaced by task and does not overwrite unrelated task data.

description: Targeted tests cover coding task storage, lifecycle transitions, and prompt-building helpers without relying on the repo-wide red baseline.
steps: Run the focused coding task test suite.
Verify the suite covers store persistence, status transitions, and prompt-shaping logic.
Confirm the focused suite passes even while the unrelated repo_sync baseline remains red.

description: Targeted tests cover restart recovery and missing-session failure handling for recoverable coding tasks.
steps: Create recoverable and broken coding task fixtures.
Run the recovery-focused tests.
Verify live sessions reconnect while missing sessions are marked failed with recovery hints.

description: Targeted tests cover Telegram command parsing and control-message routing for coding tasks.
steps: Run the Telegram command-parsing test suite for coding tasks.
Verify `开始编程`, `状态`, `继续`, `停止`, and `取消` are all routed correctly.
Confirm ordinary chat messages still bypass the coding-task control path.

description: Repository documentation explains nanobot's role as orchestrator, Codex's role as coding worker, and the target repo harness as the long-term memory layer.
steps: Open the updated docs or architecture section after implementation.
Verify it explains the three-layer split between nanobot, Codex, and repo harness.
Confirm it includes the user-visible control flow for Telegram-driven coding tasks.

description: Progress notes record the known unrelated repo_sync baseline failure separately from coding-task verification so later sessions do not misdiagnose it.
steps: Open `PROGRESS.md` after the coding-task session updates.
Verify it names the missing `nanobot.repo_sync.service` baseline issue separately from coding-task tests.
Confirm later sessions can rerun the focused coding-task verification command directly from the notes.

description: Gateway runtime keeps the existing single-instance safety checks while adding coding-task orchestration so duplicate gateways do not duplicate coding work.
steps: Attempt to start a second gateway process while one is already active.
Verify the duplicate process is rejected before coding-task orchestration starts.
Confirm the original gateway retains ownership of tracked coding tasks.

description: An end-to-end manual run can start a real coding task against a local repo, launch Codex, persist state, and return a status summary through nanobot.
steps: Launch a real coding task against a local test repo from the nanobot entrypoint.
Verify Codex starts in the target repo, reads or initializes the harness, and updates task state on disk.
Confirm nanobot can report the live task status back through its user-facing control path.
